title: Around the World in Eighty Days
author: Jules Verne
overture: A wager at the Reform Club sends Phileas Fogg eastward round the world against the clock - by rail over the Mont Cenis, by P&O steamer through Suez and the Indian Ocean, by elephant across the Indian forests, by packet to Japan, across the Pacific and the American plains (a wind-sledge among them), and home over the Atlantic in a ship he burns for fuel. Four travellers, one detective's pursuit, and a day quietly won at the meridian.

chapters.title: Fogg and Passepartout accept each other
Passepartout finds his ideal
A conversation likely to cost Fogg dear
Fogg astounds Passepartout
A new species of funds appears on 'Change
Fix betrays a very natural impatience
The uselessness of passports
Passepartout talks rather more than is prudent
The Red Sea and the Indian Ocean prove propitious
Passepartout gets off with the loss of his shoes
A curious means of conveyance at a fabulous price
Across the Indian forests, and what ensued
Fortune favours the brave
Down the valley of the Ganges
The bag of banknotes disgorges thousands more
Fix does not seem to understand
From Singapore to Hong Kong
Each about his business
Passepartout takes too great an interest
Fix comes face to face with Fogg
The master of the 'Tankadere'
Even at the antipodes it helps to have money
Passepartout's nose becomes outrageously long
Crossing the Pacific Ocean
A slight glimpse of San Francisco
By the Pacific Railroad
A course of Mormon history
Passepartout cannot make anybody listen
Incidents met with only on American railroads
Fogg simply does his duty
Fix furthers the interests of Fogg
A direct struggle with bad fortune
Fogg shows himself equal to the occasion
Fogg at last reaches London
Fogg does not repeat his orders twice
Fogg's name once more at a premium
Fogg gained nothing, unless it were happiness

characters.name: Phileas Fogg
characters.role: A precise, imperturbable English gentleman of the Reform Club; the spine of the wager and the race

characters.name: Jean Passepartout
characters.role: Fogg's French servant, hired that same morning; loyal, resourceful and forever the accident that nearly loses the bet

characters.name: Detective Fix
characters.role: A Scotland Yard detective who takes Fogg for the Bank of England robber and shadows him round the world, warrant always just out of reach

exit.note: The last the book sees of Fix, he is flat on the floor of the Liverpool Custom House, knocked down by the one rapid motion Fogg ever makes. Verne then says it outright: 'The police were ignorant what had become of the detective, Fix.' He is talked of afterwards, and given a share of the winnings, but never placed anywhere again.

characters.name: Aouda
characters.role: A young Parsee widow rescued from a suttee near Pillaji; travels with Fogg's party thereafter, and at the last marries him

locations.name: Reform Club, Pall Mall, London
locations.story: Where the wager is struck over whist and the Daily Telegraph's timetable, and where - eighty days later - Fogg must be back by a quarter before nine to win. He leaves at twenty-five past seven that same evening.
locations.quote:
I shall be due in London in this very room of the Reform Club, on
Saturday, the 21st of December, at a quarter before nine p.m.

image.caption: The Reform Club, Pall Mall, wood engraving, 1896
image.credit: Wood engraving, 1896 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Charing Cross station, London
locations.story: The party takes the evening train for the Continent, bound over the Channel for Paris and the Mont Cenis road to Brindisi. The race has begun.
locations.quote: Phileas Fogg, at twenty-five minutes past seven, left the Reform Club
locations.imageBlank: No clean period image of Charing Cross's main-line departure platform surfaced; the one etching found is of the underground frontage, heavily annotated in its mount - a logged blank beats a mislabelled print.

locations.name: Paris
locations.story: A crossing of the city between stations on the run south to the Alps; Passepartout barely glimpses it.
locations.quote:
each principal point Paris, Brindisi,
Suez, Bombay, Calcutta, Singapore, Hong Kong, Yokohama, San Francisco,
New York, and London

image.caption: The Boulevard Montmartre, Paris, c.1870
image.credit: Art Institute of Chicago · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Turin
locations.story: Reached by the Mont Cenis tunnel and pass on the morning of Friday 4 October - the Alps crossed, Italy ahead.
locations.quote: Reached Turin by Mont Cenis, Friday, October 4th, at 6.35 a.m.

image.caption: Turin, the Piazza Castello, c.1880
image.credit: Hallwyl Museum, c.1880 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Mont Cenis Pass
locations.story: The Alpine crossing between France and Italy named in the Telegraph's itinerary; the Fréjus (Mont Cenis) railway tunnel had opened only the year before, in 1871.
locations.note: The itinerary names 'Mont Cenis' as the Alpine crossing; this point sits on the pass/tunnel line between Modane and Bardonecchia. Verne means the new Fréjus rail tunnel, opened 1871.
locations.quote:
From London to Suez _viâ_ Mont Cenis and Brindisi, by rail and
steamboats

image.caption: The Mont Cenis (Fell) mountain railway at Grand Croix, 1871
image.credit: Giacomo Brogi, 1871 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Brindisi
locations.story: The Adriatic railhead where the P&O steamer Mongolia takes on the Indian mails. Fogg boards on the Saturday afternoon and sails at five.
locations.quote: Arrived at Brindisi, Saturday, October 5th, at 4 p.m.

image.caption: Brindisi railway station, the P&O railhead, c.1870
image.credit: c.1870 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Suez
locations.story: At the head of the Red Sea, where Fix waits with a warrant that never comes and inspects the Mongolia's passengers. Passepartout has his passport stamped; the detective attaches himself to the party here.
locations.quote: Reached Suez, Wednesday, October 9th, at 11 a.m.

image.caption: The opening of the Suez Canal at Ismailia, 1869
image.credit: Stereoview, 1869 · Rijksmuseum · CC0

locations.name: Aden
locations.story: The Mongolia's coaling stop at the mouth of the Red Sea. Fogg and his servant go ashore for four hours to have the passport stamped again; the ship makes up her lost time and steams on for Bombay.
locations.quote:
Mr. Fogg and his servant went ashore at Aden to have the passport again
_visaed;_

image.caption: Aden, Steamer Point, 1855–56
image.credit: Auguste Bartholdi, 1855–56 · Bibliothèque nationale de France · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Bombay (Mumbai)
locations.story: India reached ahead of time. Passepartout wanders into a pagoda in his shoes and is set upon; the Great Indian Peninsula Railway carries them east that same evening - and that small blasphemy will hand Fix his pretext later.
locations.quote: at the quays of Bombay

image.caption: Bombay harbour, photographed c.1870
image.credit: Albumen photograph, c.1870 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Kholby (near Kholapur, Deccan)
locations.note: Kholby is Verne's invented railhead where the unfinished line stops, 'fifteen miles beyond Rothal' in the Sahpoor jungle. No such village exists; this point is an editorial best guess in the Western Ghats interior on the line's eastward course, before the country climbs to the Allahabad plain.
locations.story: Here the railway simply stops: fifty miles of track never laid, though the tickets are sold through. Fogg buys an elephant, Kiouni, at a fabulous price, hires a guide, and sets off across the forest for Allahabad.
locations.quote: At the hamlet of Kholby.

image.caption: A bullock cart on a forest track in India - an indicative view of the central-Indian jungle the railway crossed, c.1900
image.credit: c.1900 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Pillaji (Bundelcund forest)
locations.note: The pagoda of Pillaji is Verne's invention, set in the forest country between Kholby and Allahabad. This point is an editorial best guess in the Bundelcund/Vindhya jungle the elephant crosses, short of Allahabad.
locations.story: Deep in the forest the party comes on a suttee: the young widow Aouda drugged with opium and hemp, to be burned at dawn beside her dead rajah. At the last moment Passepartout, hidden on the pyre, rises as the dead prince and carries her off.
locations.quote:
To the pagoda of Pillaji, two miles from here; she will pass the night
there

image.caption: The Great Pagoda at Tanjore, 1798 - an indicative view of a South Indian Hindu pagoda
image.credit: Thomas & William Daniell, 1798 · British Library · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Allahabad (Prayagraj)
locations.story: Where the rails begin again. The elephant is given to the guide as reward; the party takes the train down the Ganges valley, buying European clothes for Aouda before boarding.
locations.quote: the streets of Allahabad, that is, the City of God

image.caption: The Ganges ghat below Allahabad Fort, photographed c.1858–61
image.credit: Metropolitan Museum of Art, c.1858–61 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Benares (Varanasi)
locations.story: Sir Francis Cromarty's destination, where the brigadier leaves the party. The train presses on down the Ganges for Calcutta.
locations.quote: At half-past twelve the train stopped at Benares.

image.caption: The burning ghats and Aurangzeb’s mosque on the Ganges at Benares, c.1860
image.credit: Rijksmuseum, c.1850–1900 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Calcutta (Kolkata)
locations.story: The party is haled before a magistrate over the Bombay pagoda - Fix's doing - and Fogg buys their way free with bail forfeited. They reach the steamer Rangoon with minutes to spare, bound for Hong Kong by way of Singapore.
locations.quote:
Calcutta was reached at seven in the morning, and the packet left for
Hong Kong at noon

image.caption: Shipping on the Hooghly river at Calcutta, c.1860
image.credit: Francis Frith, c.1850–1870 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Singapore
locations.story: A coaling call on the Rangoon's run to Hong Kong. A few hours ashore among nutmeg groves and clove plantations, then out past the mountains of Malacca.
locations.quote: the “Rangoon” rode out of Singapore harbour

image.caption: Singapore harbour, c.1880
image.credit: Rijksmuseum, c.1870–1900 · Wikimedia Commons · CC0

locations.name: Hong Kong
locations.story: The last British ground before Japan - where Fix's warrant might at last be valid, and is not. He gets Passepartout drunk and drugged in an opium den so he cannot warn Fogg; the Carnatic sails a day early, carrying the servant off senseless and leaving master and detective behind. Fogg hires the pilot-boat Tankadere to chase her.
locations.quote: Hong Kong, which is a little English colony near the Chinese coast

image.caption: Victoria Harbour, Hong Kong, from the Botanical Gardens, c.1870
image.credit: William Pryor Floyd, c.1870 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Shanghai
locations.story: The Tankadere's true goal: not Yokohama itself but Shanghai, where the American mail steamer for San Francisco calls. Through a gale the little schooner catches the General Grant off the coast and signals her down.
locations.quote: Shanghai we should not be forced to sail wide of the Chinese coast,

image.caption: The Bund, Shanghai, c.1869
image.credit: John Thomson, c.1869 · Metropolitan Museum of Art · Wikimedia Commons · CC0

locations.name: Yokohama
locations.story: Passepartout wakes here aboard the Carnatic, penniless and alone, and joins Batulcar's acrobatic troupe of Long Noses to earn his passage. Fogg arrives on the General Grant and finds his servant at the bottom of a human pyramid; the party, reunited, sails for America.
locations.quote:
ACROBATIC JAPANESE TROUPE,
HONOURABLE WILLIAM BATULCAR, PROPRIETOR

image.caption: Yokohama, the treaty port, c.1865–70
image.credit: Rijksmuseum, c.1860–80 · Wikimedia Commons · CC0

locations.name: San Francisco
locations.story: America, reached on 3 December after twenty-two days of Pacific. A political brawl in the street, a first sight of the New World - and that evening the party boards the Pacific Railroad train east.
locations.quote:
the “General Grant” entered
the bay of the Golden Gate, and reached San Francisco.

image.caption: San Francisco from Nob Hill - from Muybridge’s panorama, 1878
image.credit: Eadweard Muybridge, 1878 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Ogden, Utah
locations.story: Where the Central Pacific hands the line to the Union Pacific, and the party breaks the journey to run up the branch to Salt Lake City for a glimpse of the Mormon capital.
locations.quote: The train reached Ogden at two o’clock

image.caption: Ogden, Utah, on the Pacific Railroad, c.1870
image.credit: C. R. Savage, c.1870 · Brigham Young University · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Great Salt Lake, Utah
locations.story: The railroad rounds the great inland sea of the Mormons. A fellow passenger delivers Passepartout a whole lecture on Mormon history at twenty miles an hour.
locations.quote:
turns the Great Salt Lake, and reaches Salt Lake
City

image.caption: A railroad trestle in the Great Salt Lake valley at Promontory, 1868
image.credit: A. J. Russell, 1868 · Library of Congress · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Medicine Bow, Wyoming
locations.story: A failing suspension bridge over a torrent. The engineer takes it at full speed to jump the gap; the train clears it and the bridge falls into the rapids behind.
locations.quote: The bridge at Medicine Bow is shaky

image.caption: A. J. Russell's photograph of the Union Pacific's high timber trestle at Dale Creek, Wyoming, c.1868 - indicative of the shaky bridge Fogg's train clears at full speed near Medicine Bow.
image.credit: Andrew J. Russell · Beinecke Library, Yale · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Plum Creek, Nebraska
locations.story: On the plains a war-party of Sioux storms the train. In the running fight Passepartout uncouples the locomotive to stop the cars at the fort - and is carried off by the Indians.
locations.quote: Do you know Plum Creek?

image.caption: A Union Pacific station on the Nebraska plains in 1869, freight cars at the platform - indicative of the small prairie stops like Plum Creek, where a Sioux war-party storms Fogg's train.
image.credit: U.S. Geological Survey, 1869 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Fort Kearny, Nebraska
locations.note: Verne spells it 'Fort Kearney'; the real post on the Platte is Fort Kearny, near present Kearney, Nebraska. This point is at the fort's site on the south bank of the Platte.
locations.story: The train halts at the fort for protection. Fogg gives up the wager without hesitation to lead a party of soldiers after his servant - and rescues him. But the train has gone on without them; they are stranded, and must find another way to Omaha.
locations.quote: The commanding officer of Fort Kearney was there.

image.caption: The stage station at Fort Kearny on the Platte, drawn in 1863 with its coaches for Omaha - the post where Fogg leaves the wager to rescue Passepartout, and is stranded, needing another way east.
image.credit: 1863 lithograph · U.S. National Archives · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Omaha, Nebraska
locations.story: The eastern terminus of the Pacific Railroad proper, reached by wind-sledge across the frozen prairie after the train was lost. From here fast trains run through Chicago to the Atlantic seaboard.
locations.quote:
The Pacific Railroad proper finds its terminus at this important
Nebraska town.

image.caption: Omaha, Nebraska, eastern terminus of the Pacific Railroad, c.1868
image.credit: Jackson Brothers, c.1865–70 · New York Public Library · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Chicago, Illinois
locations.story: Reached on the 10th, 'already risen from its ruins' a year after the Great Fire. Fogg changes trains at once for the run east to New York.
locations.quote:
it reached Chicago,
already risen from its ruins

image.caption: Chicago from Lake Michigan in an 1874 Currier & Ives bird's-eye view - the city already risen from its ruins, where Fogg changes at once for the run east to New York.
image.credit: Currier & Ives, 1874 · Library of Congress · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: New York
locations.story: They reach the quay forty-five minutes after the Liverpool steamer China has sailed. Fogg charters the trading vessel Henrietta instead, buys off her crew, and - when her captain refuses Liverpool - takes command himself.
locations.quote:
The “China,” for Liverpool, had started three-quarters of an hour
before!

image.caption: New York harbour and the East River, Currier & Ives, 1879
image.credit: C.R. Parsons for Currier & Ives, 1879 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Queenstown (Cobh), Ireland
locations.story: The Henrietta, her upperworks burned for fuel, raises the Irish coast and Fastnet Light. Rather than crawl the last miles to Liverpool on failing steam, Fogg lands here to take the mail express - the trick that gains twelve hours on the Atlantic steamers.
locations.quote:
By ten
in the evening they were passing Queenstown.

image.caption: Queenstown (Cobh) harbour, Co. Cork, c.1890
image.credit: Detroit Publishing Co. photochrom, c.1890–1900 · Library of Congress · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Dublin
locations.story: The mail train runs them up from Queenstown by dawn, and they embark at once on the fast Kingstown packet across the Irish Sea for Liverpool.
locations.quote: at dawn of day they were in Dublin

image.caption: Sackville Street and O’Connell Bridge, Dublin, c.1890
image.credit: Detroit Publishing Co. photochrom, c.1890–1900 · Library of Congress · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Liverpool
locations.story: Fogg steps ashore at twenty minutes to noon on the 21st, six hours from London and seemingly the winner - and there Fix, warrant in hand at last, arrests him. The lost hours in the Custom House will appear to cost him everything.
locations.quote:
Phileas Fogg at last disembarked on the Liverpool quay, at twenty
minutes before twelve, 21st December.

image.caption: Liverpool dockside on the Mersey, c.1897
image.credit: Liverpool Record Office, c.1897 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: London (return)
locations.story: Freed once Fix realises his blunder, Fogg races for London believing himself five minutes too late. But travelling always eastward he has gained a full day at the meridian: it is not Sunday but Saturday, and he reaches the Reform Club with seconds to spare.
locations.quote:
Phileas Fogg had won his wager, and had made his journey around the
world in eighty days.

image.caption: The Houses of Parliament and the Thames, c.1890
image.credit: Detroit Publishing Co. photochrom, c.1890–1900 · Library of Congress · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

movements.note: Out of the Reform Club at twenty-five past seven, by cab to the station

movements.routeNote: The overland mail route to the Indian steamers: London to Paris, south to the Mont Cenis pass and tunnel, down the length of Italy to Brindisi, where P&O took on the mails.
movements.routeSource: The Mont Cenis rail route and the line down the Adriatic coast of Italy - Bologna, Ancona, Bari - to Brindisi.
movements.note: The overland dash across Europe to catch the Mongolia

movements.routeNote: The P&O steamer Mongolia from Brindisi across the Mediterranean to Port Said and down the newly opened Suez Canal to Suez.
movements.routeSource: The P&O Mediterranean lane, keeping south of the Peloponnese and Crete to Port Said.
movements.note: Aboard the P&O steamer Mongolia, through the Med and the Canal

movements.routeNote: The Mongolia's Red Sea and Arabian Sea passage, coaling at Aden, then across to Bombay - the standard P&O India run.
movements.routeSource: Down the centre of the Red Sea, out at Bab-el-Mandeb by Aden, across the Arabian Sea.
movements.note: The Red Sea and the Indian Ocean prove propitious

movements.routeNote: Fix boards the Mongolia at Suez and follows Fogg on the same P&O steamer to Bombay, hoping a warrant will overtake them there.
movements.routeSource: Down the centre of the Red Sea, out at Bab-el-Mandeb by Aden, across the Arabian Sea.
movements.note: The detective attaches himself to the chase

via.name: Poona (Pune)
via.note: The Great Indian Peninsula Railway climbs the Western Ghats above Bombay toward the Deccan plateau.

via.name: The Deccan interior
via.note: East across the tableland toward the forest country where the finished line stops.

movements.routeNote: The Great Indian Peninsula Railway east out of Bombay across the Western Ghats and the Deccan - as far as the line was actually laid, which stopped short in the jungle.
movements.routeSource: Verne, Ch. 11–12: the GIPR route Bombay–Allahabad, with the gap 'between Rothal and Allahabad' where 'the line begins again'; the general route is given in Ch. 12.
movements.note: By rail into the interior - until the track simply ends

via.name: Poona (Pune)
via.note: Fix, unable to arrest Fogg, keeps to the through railway across the Ghats.

via.name: Allahabad
via.note: Where the completed line resumes; Fix rides the rails the whole way rather than the elephant road.

movements.routeNote: Fix stays with the Great Indian Peninsula Railway the whole way to Calcutta, shadowing Fogg's party to their re-embarkation while nursing his Bombay-pagoda pretext.
movements.routeSource: Verne, Ch. 13–15: Fix follows to Calcutta and engineers the party's arrest there over the Bombay pagoda; GIPR Bombay–Allahabad–Calcutta.
movements.note: The detective keeps to the rails, biding his moment

via.name: The Sahpoor jungle
via.note: The guide steers the elephant Kiouni off the road and into deep forest to shorten the way and avoid the villages.

movements.routeNote: The elephant crossing of the Indian forests between the broken railhead and the resuming line - struck cross-country through the Bundelcund jungle, where the party comes on the suttee at Pillaji.
movements.routeSource: Verne, Ch. 11–12: Fogg buys the elephant at Kholby and crosses 'the Indian forests' toward Allahabad; the pagoda of Pillaji lies on the way. Reconstructed: the villages are invented and the exact track unnamed.
movements.note: Across the forests by elephant - and the fires of Pillaji

via.name: The Bundelcund forest
via.note: The last stretch of jungle north-east toward the plain, Aouda carried insensible among them.

movements.routeNote: The elephant carries the party on out of the forest to the railhead at Allahabad, the rescued Aouda with them.
movements.routeSource: Verne, Ch. 13: from Pillaji the elephant makes for Allahabad, 'now only twelve miles to the north-east'. Reconstructed: the forest track is unnamed.
movements.note: Out of the forest with Aouda, to where the rails resume

via.name: Benares
via.note: Sir Francis Cromarty's destination; the line runs down the valley of the Ganges.

via.name: The Ganges valley
via.note: The railway follows the great river east across the plain of Bengal.

via.name: Burdwan approaches
via.note: The line bends south-east off the river for the run down to Calcutta.

movements.routeNote: The East Indian Railway down the valley of the Ganges - Allahabad, Benares, and on across Bengal to Calcutta.
movements.routeSource: Verne, Ch. 14 ('Down the valley of the Ganges'): the train runs Allahabad–Benares–Calcutta; the East Indian Railway's Ganges-valley main line.
movements.note: Down the Ganges to Calcutta - and a magistrate's court

movements.routeNote: The P&O steamer Rangoon from Calcutta down the Bay of Bengal, through the Strait of Malacca and Singapore, and up the South China Sea to Hong Kong.
movements.routeSource: The mail lane through the Strait of Malacca to Singapore, then north-east across the open South China Sea.
movements.note: Aboard the Rangoon, by Singapore to the China coast

movements.routeNote: Fix travels on the same Rangoon to Hong Kong, gambling that his warrant will reach the last British port before Fogg leaves it.
movements.routeSource: The mail lane through the Strait of Malacca to Singapore, then north-east across the open South China Sea.
movements.note: The pursuit, still one warrant short

movements.routeNote: Passepartout's solo leg: drugged in the Hong Kong opium den, he is carried aboard the Carnatic and wakes at Yokohama alone, separated from Fogg.
movements.routeSource: East of Formosa through the Bashi Channel, then across the Pacific south of Japan to Yokohama.
movements.note: Separated: carried off drugged aboard the Carnatic

movements.routeNote: Having missed the Carnatic, Fogg charters the pilot-schooner Tankadere to run up the coast to Shanghai and intercept the San Francisco mail steamer there.
movements.routeSource: Up the coast well offshore in the East China Sea to the mouth of the Yangtze.
movements.note: The Tankadere's storm-run to catch the American steamer

movements.routeNote: Fix takes passage on the same Tankadere; his interest has flipped - he now needs Fogg to reach Britain, where the warrant will finally bite.
movements.routeSource: Up the coast well offshore in the East China Sea to the mouth of the Yangtze.
movements.note: The shadow, now willing the chase along

movements.routeNote: Picked up by the Pacific Mail steamer General Grant off Shanghai, the party crosses to Yokohama, where Fogg finds his lost servant.
movements.routeSource: Across the East China Sea and the Pacific south of Honshu into Sagami Bay.
movements.note: Aboard the General Grant to Yokohama - and Passepartout found

via.name: The western Pacific
via.note: Out of Yokohama east-north-east into the open ocean, the party reunited aboard.

via.name: The mid-Pacific
via.note: The great-circle track curving up toward the north Pacific.

via.name: The 180th meridian
via.note: Crossing the International Date Line eastward - the day quietly gained that will win the wager.

via.name: The eastern Pacific
via.note: West longitude now; the track bends down toward the American coast.

via.name: Off the American coast
via.note: The long descent south-east toward the Golden Gate.

movements.routeNote: The Pacific Mail steamer General Grant's great-circle crossing from Yokohama to San Francisco, over the 180th meridian - the eastward passage that gains a day. Laid west-to-east across the date line (139° → 180° → −122°), not backward across Asia.
movements.routeSource: Verne, Ch. 24: twenty-two days Yokohama–San Francisco, reached 3 December; the crossing spans 'exactly one half of the terrestrial globe'. Pacific Mail Steamship Company Yokohama–San Francisco line.
movements.note: The Pacific crossing - and the meridian's hidden gift

via.name: Sacramento
via.note: The Central Pacific's starting-point; the line runs north-east from the bay.

via.name: The Sierra Nevada
via.note: Climbing the range by Colfax and Cisco, over the summit toward the Nevada desert.

via.name: Reno
via.note: Down onto the Great American Desert, east across Nevada.

movements.routeNote: The Central Pacific from San Francisco: north-east by Sacramento, up over the Sierra Nevada, and across the Nevada desert to Ogden, where the Union Pacific takes over.
movements.routeSource: Verne, Ch. 25–27: the Central Pacific Sacramento–Sierra Nevada–Reno–Ogden; 'the Central Pacific, between San Francisco and Ogden'. The 1869 transcontinental line.
movements.note: Up over the Sierra by the Pacific Railroad

via.name: Great Salt Lake
via.note: The Union Pacific turns the great inland sea; a branch runs down to the Mormon capital.

via.name: Green River
via.note: A snowy halt in Wyoming, the line climbing the Rockies.

via.name: Medicine Bow
via.note: The failing bridge, taken at a run; it falls into the rapids behind the train.

movements.routeNote: The Union Pacific east from Ogden: round the Great Salt Lake, up over the Rockies by Green River and Medicine Bow, and down onto the Nebraska plains toward Fort Kearny - until the Sioux attack halts them.
movements.routeSource: Verne, Ch. 26–30: the Union Pacific 'between Ogden and Omaha', Great Salt Lake, Green River, Medicine Bow bridge, the Sioux attack near Plum Creek, Fort Kearny. The 1869 Union Pacific line.
movements.note: East across the Rockies and plains - to a war-party of Sioux
Carried off by the Sioux in the running fight - Fogg gives up the wager to go after him
Rescued by Fogg and the soldiers, brought back to the fort - but the train has gone

via.name: The frozen Platte prairie
via.note: The wind-sledge takes a chord across the arc the railway describes, straight over the hardened snow.

via.name: The Platte River
via.note: Crossed near noon; Mudge knows he is within twenty miles of Omaha.

movements.routeNote: Having missed the train, the party crosses the frozen prairie by wind-sledge - a sail-rigged land-craft - cutting a straight chord across the railway's curve to Omaha.
movements.routeSource: Verne, Ch. 32: Mudge's sail-sledge from below Fort Kearny to Omaha, 'shortening this route, took a chord of the arc described by the railway', crossing the Platte near noon.
movements.note: The wind-sledge, flying over the snow to Omaha

via.name: Council Bluffs
via.note: Across the Missouri into Iowa on the Chicago & Rock Island road.

via.name: Davenport
via.note: The Mississippi crossed by night at Rock Island, into Illinois.

via.name: Chicago
via.note: Reached on the 10th, 'already risen from its ruins'; straight onto the eastern line.

via.name: Pittsburgh, Fort Wayne & Chicago line
via.note: Across Indiana, Ohio, Pennsylvania and New Jersey 'like a flash' to the Hudson.

movements.routeNote: The eastern railroads from Omaha: the Chicago & Rock Island through Iowa to Chicago, then the Pittsburgh, Fort Wayne & Chicago line across to New York.
movements.routeSource: Verne, Ch. 32: Omaha–Council Bluffs–Davenport–Chicago (Chicago & Rock Island), then Chicago–New York (Pittsburgh, Fort Wayne & Chicago Railway).
movements.note: The last American rails - but the Liverpool steamer already gone

via.name: The western Atlantic
via.note: The chartered Henrietta stands out east; Fogg has locked her captain in his cabin and taken command.

via.name: The mid-Atlantic
via.note: Coal running short, the crew begin burning the ship's own upperworks for fuel.

via.name: The eastern Atlantic
via.note: The Henrietta a flat hulk, driving for the Irish coast and Fastnet Light.

movements.routeNote: The Henrietta, chartered and then commandeered, crosses the Atlantic from New York toward Liverpool - but with the coal spent and her woodwork burned for steam, she makes for the nearer Irish coast at Queenstown.
movements.routeSource: Verne, Ch. 32–33: Fogg buys the Henrietta at New York, burns her upperworks for fuel, sights 'the Irish coast and Fastnet Light' and passes Queenstown by ten in the evening on the 20th.
movements.note: The Henrietta burned to her waterline to make the crossing

movements.routeNote: Fogg's twelve-hour trick: land the mails at Queenstown, express train to Dublin, and the fast packet across the Irish Sea to Liverpool - beating the Atlantic steamers into port.
movements.routeSource: Round the south of Ireland and up St George's Channel and the Irish Sea to the Mersey.
movements.note: The mail route by Dublin - twelve hours gained, and then arrest

via.name: Manchester approaches
via.note: The London & North Western main line east out of Liverpool.

via.name: Rugby
via.note: Down the West Coast trunk toward the capital; the special engine flying.

via.name: Euston
via.note: The London terminus, reached with minutes to spare for the Reform Club.

movements.routeNote: Freed from arrest, Fogg charters a special train down the London & North Western main line from Liverpool to Euston, racing for the Reform Club - arriving, by the day gained at the meridian, just in time.
movements.routeSource: Verne, Ch. 34–35: released, Fogg takes a special train Liverpool–London; the London & North Western West Coast main line to Euston. The eastward day-gain is resolved in Ch. 37.
movements.note: The last dash to London - and the day quietly won

story.title: The wager
story.narration: At the Reform Club the precise, unhurried Phileas Fogg wagers his whole fortune - twenty thousand pounds - that he can circle the globe in eighty days. He must be back in this very room by a quarter before nine on 21 December.

story.title: By cab to the train
story.narration: That same evening Fogg takes a cab to the station with his new French servant, Passepartout, hired only that morning and already bewildered. A carpet-bag of banknotes is the whole of the luggage the wager allows.

story.title: Across Europe
story.narration: The overland dash to catch the Indian mail: across the Channel, through Paris, over the Alps by the new Mont Cenis tunnel, and down the length of Italy to the Adriatic port of Brindisi, where the P&O steamer waits.

story.title: The Mongolia
story.narration: Aboard the mail steamer Mongolia, out across the Mediterranean to Port Said and down the newly cut Suez Canal. Fogg plays whist below while the miles fall quietly away behind him.

story.title: The detective
story.narration: Watching the Suez quay is Detective Fix of Scotland Yard. The Bank of England has just been robbed of fifty-five thousand pounds, and this cool, well-dressed traveller matches the thief's description exactly. Fix means to arrest him.
Follow the detective's own thread first, from Suez, a little before Fogg's steamer sails on for India.

story.title: The shadow embarks
story.narration: No warrant has reached him, so Fix boards the Mongolia himself and follows down the Red Sea toward India, wiring ahead for the papers that would let him take Fogg on British soil the moment the ship makes Bombay.
And the man he hunts, unknowing, on that same deck? Take up Fogg's own thread again from Suez, the detective at his heels.

story.title: The Red Sea run
story.narration: Down the Red Sea and across the Arabian Sea, coaling at Aden. Fair winds help the engines, and the Mongolia reaches Bombay two full days ahead of the timetable - the wager running, for now, in Fogg's favour.

story.title: Into the interior
story.narration: Passepartout blunders into a Bombay pagoda still shod and is set upon - a small sacrilege Fix will store up. That evening the railway carries them east into the Deccan, until the track simply ends in the jungle at the hamlet of Kholby.

story.title: By elephant
story.narration: Fifty miles of line were never laid. Unruffled, Fogg buys an elephant named Kiouni at a fabulous price, hires a guide, and the party strikes cross-country through the forest - toward a glow of torches at the pagoda of Pillaji.

story.title: The pyre
story.narration: At Pillaji a suttee: a young Parsee widow, Aouda, drugged to be burned alive beside her dead rajah. Passepartout, hidden among the logs, rises from the pyre as the prince seeming to live and snatches her free. The elephant bears all three on to Allahabad.

story.title: Down the Ganges
story.narration: The rails resume. Kiouni is given to the faithful guide, Aouda is dressed in European clothes, and the train runs down the valley of the Ganges - past Benares, where their soldier companion leaves them - to Calcutta on the sea.

story.title: The magistrate
story.narration: In Calcutta the party is haled before a magistrate - Fix's doing, the Bombay pagoda his pretext. Fogg forfeits two thousand pounds in bail without a flicker and walks free, reaching the Hong Kong steamer with only minutes to spare.

story.title: The Rangoon
story.narration: Aboard the steamer Rangoon, down the Bay of Bengal and through the Strait of Malacca, a few hours ashore at Singapore among the clove groves, then up the South China Sea to Hong Kong through the first of the monsoon weather.
And the detective, all this while? Wind back to India and follow Fix's own dogged pursuit, which never once left the rails.

story.title: Fix keeps to the rails
story.narration: He had no elephant to hire, and so stayed on the through railway across the Ghats and down the Ganges to Calcutta, nursing his pagoda charge and biding the moment his warrant from London would finally overtake him.

story.title: The last British ground
story.narration: Then aboard the same Rangoon, east to Hong Kong. It is the final stretch of British soil before Japan; there, Fix is certain, his warrant will catch up at last, and Fogg's flight round the world will end on a colonial quay.

story.title: The opium den
story.narration: At Hong Kong the warrant still fails. Desperate, Fix lures the loyal Passepartout into an opium den and leaves him senseless, so the servant cannot warn his master that their steamer, the Carnatic, is sailing a day early.

story.title: Carried off
story.narration: Insensible, Passepartout is carried aboard the Carnatic and wakes far out at sea - alone, penniless, adrift - the ship bearing him on toward Japan while Fogg is left stranded, servantless, on the Hong Kong quay.

story.title: The schooner
story.narration: Fogg finds his steamer gone and his servant vanished, and does not lose a moment. He hires the pilot-schooner Tankadere and her master to run him up the coast to Shanghai, where he may yet catch the American mail for the Pacific.
Wind back to the hour Passepartout was carried off, and leave Hong Kong a second time - now on Fogg's little schooner.

story.title: Through the gale
story.narration: With Aouda beside him, Fogg drives the little schooner north through a rising gale. Seas sweep her deck and the mast strains near to breaking, but the Tankadere claws up the Chinese coast, racing the clock for Shanghai.
The detective is aboard too. His Hong Kong plan in ruins, Fix has thrown in his lot with the very man he means to arrest.

story.title: Willing him on
story.narration: His interest has flipped clean over: Fix now needs Fogg to reach England, where the warrant will finally bite. And so, absurdly, the shadow shares the storm-schooner and wills its wild run up the coast along with everyone else.

story.title: Flagging down the steamer
story.narration: Off Shanghai the schooner signals the departing General Grant with flag and gun, and is answered. Aboard the American steamer at last, Fogg's party crosses to Yokohama - where his lost servant may yet be found.

story.title: The Long Noses
story.narration: And the servant himself? Marooned in Yokohama, Passepartout has sold his coat and joined a troupe of tumblers billed as the Long Noses. It is there, at the foot of a swaying human pyramid, that Fogg finds him, and the party is whole again.

story.title: The Pacific
story.narration: Reunited, all four board the General Grant for the long haul east across the Pacific. Somewhere near the 180th meridian they cross the date line travelling always eastward, and gain - unknowing - a whole day that will decide the wager.

story.title: The Pacific Railroad
story.narration: America reached on 3 December, and that evening the train east. The Central Pacific climbs from Sacramento up over the Sierra Nevada and down onto the Nevada desert, running for Ogden, where the Union Pacific takes up the line.

story.title: Across the Rockies
story.narration: The Union Pacific rounds the Great Salt Lake and climbs the Rockies. At Medicine Bow a failing bridge is taken at full speed and jumped; it collapses into the torrent behind them as the train races on toward the Nebraska plains.

story.title: The Sioux
story.narration: On the open plains a Sioux war-party storms the moving train. In the running fight Passepartout crawls beneath the cars and uncouples the engine, so the carriages may halt at Fort Kearny - and is himself carried off by the raiders.

story.title: Taken
story.narration: The Sioux bear Passepartout away along the line toward Plum Creek. Without a word of hesitation Fogg gives up the wager to save him, and leads a party of soldiers out from the fort into the snow after the raiders.

story.title: Rescued
story.narration: Fogg and the soldiers overtake the war-party and bring Passepartout back alive to Fort Kearny. But the eastbound train has long since gone on without them, and the little party is stranded on the frozen prairie.
The rescue over, the four are together again at the fort - a full day behind their vanished train. Rejoin the whole party there and press on eastward.

story.title: The wind-sledge
story.narration: A prairie man rigs a sail to a sledge, and the party flies over the hardened snow at railway speed, cutting a straight chord across the line's long curve. By noon they cross the Platte and make Omaha, and the eastern rails.

story.title: To the Atlantic
story.narration: Fast trains carry them across Iowa, over the Mississippi and through Chicago - a year on, already risen from its great fire - and on to New York. But the Liverpool steamer China has sailed three-quarters of an hour before.

story.title: The Henrietta burned
story.narration: Fogg charters a trading vessel, the Henrietta, buys off her crew and, when her captain refuses Liverpool, takes command himself. As the coal fails mid-Atlantic he buys the ship outright and burns her own upperworks for steam.

story.title: Twelve hours gained
story.narration: A flat, burnt-out hulk, the Henrietta raises the Irish coast. Rather than crawl on to Liverpool, Fogg lands at Queenstown, takes the mail express to Dublin and the fast packet over the Irish Sea - gaining twelve hours on the steamers.

story.title: Arrested
story.narration: Ashore at Liverpool at twenty to noon on the 21st, six hours from London and seemingly the winner - and there Fix, his warrant come at last, arrests him. The hours lost in the Custom House look to have ruined everything.

story.title: The last dash
story.narration: The mistake exposed in London, Fogg is freed - but too late, it seems, by minutes. A special engine flies him down the West Coast main line to Euston, and he goes quietly home, resolved to marry Aouda whatever the wager has cost.

story.title: The day at the meridian
story.narration: Then Passepartout learns the truth: travelling always eastward, they had crossed the date line and gained a day. It is not Sunday but Saturday. Fogg races to the Reform Club, arrives with seconds to spare, wins - and marries Aouda.
